***Da Vinci's Demons*** is a [historical fantasy](/wiki/Historical_fantasy "Historical fantasy") drama television series that presents a fictional account of [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s early life.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-2) The series was conceived by [David S. Goyer](/wiki/David_S._Goyer "David S. Goyer") and stars [Tom Riley](/wiki/Tom_Riley_(actor) "Tom Riley (actor)") in the title role.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It was developed and produced in collaboration with [BBC Worldwide](/wiki/BBC_Worldwide "BBC Worldwide") and was [shot](/wiki/Shot_(filmmaking) "Shot (filmmaking)") in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The series has been distributed to over 120 countries.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The show follows Leonardo as he is implicated in the political schemes of the [Medici](/wiki/House_of_Medici "House of Medici") and [Pazzi](/wiki/Pazzi "Pazzi") families and their contrasting relationships with the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). These events occur alongside Leonardo's quest to obtain a mystical text called the *Book of Leaves*, which leads him to become entangled with a cult known as the [Sons of Mithras](/wiki/Mithraic_mysteries "Mithraic mysteries").

The series premiered in the United States on [Starz](/wiki/Starz_(TV_channel) "Starz (TV channel)") on 12 April 2013, and its second season premiered on 22 March 2014.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The series was renewed for a third season, which premiered on 24 October 2015.[[7]](#cite_note-season3-7) On 23 July 2015, Starz announced that the third season would be the show's last.[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, Goyer has left it open for a [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries") return.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Da_Vinci%27s_Demons&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

A fictionalised story based on historical persons, the series explores the early life of Leonardo da Vinci during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). He is an eccentric genius who has struggled to deal with his inner demons and unruly imagination, as he yearns for acceptance from his estranged father. Their sometimes antagonistic relationship results in Leonardo's working for the [House of Medici](/wiki/House_of_Medici "House of Medici"). While doing so, he becomes embroiled in a political scheme to control [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), as he hunts for a spy who is revealing information to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and the [Pazzi](/wiki/Pazzi "Pazzi") family. He also begins an affair with [Lucrezia Donati](/wiki/Lucrezia_Donati "Lucrezia Donati"), [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici")'s mistress. The series depicts many of Leonardo's inventions and subsequent works as a military engineer for the [Duke of Milan](/wiki/Ludovico_Sforza "Ludovico Sforza") and the [Borgias](/wiki/Cesare_Borgia#Borgia_and_Leonardo "Cesare Borgia").

These events coincide with Leonardo's quest to uncover the *Book of Leaves*, which a mysterious cult known as the [Sons of Mithras](/wiki/Mithraism "Mithraism") also consider very important. Mystics and the cult guide him to unlock the 'hidden areas of his mind' by accessing the *Fountain of Memory*, and inform him that he has the power to see the future and also to shape it.

* [Laura Haddock](/wiki/Laura_Haddock "Laura Haddock") as [Lucrezia Donati](/wiki/Lucrezia_Donati "Lucrezia Donati")
* [Blake Ritson](/wiki/Blake_Ritson "Blake Ritson") as Count [Girolamo Riario](/wiki/Girolamo_Riario "Girolamo Riario")
* [Elliot Cowan](/wiki/Elliot_Cowan "Elliot Cowan") as [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici")

* [Allan Corduner](/wiki/Allan_Corduner "Allan Corduner") as [Andrea Verrocchio](/wiki/Andrea_del_Verrocchio "Andrea del Verrocchio") (seasons 1–2)
* [Michael Elwyn](/wiki/Michael_Elwyn "Michael Elwyn") as Gentile Becchi (season 1)
* [Michael Culkin](/wiki/Michael_Culkin "Michael Culkin") as Jacopo Pazzi (seasons 1–2)
* [Nick Dunning](/wiki/Nick_Dunning "Nick Dunning") as Cardinal Lupo Mercuri, Curator of the [Vatican Secret Archives](/wiki/Vatican_Secret_Archives "Vatican Secret Archives")
* [Elliot Levey](/wiki/Elliot_Levey "Elliot Levey") as [Francesco de' Pazzi](/wiki/Francesco_de%27_Pazzi "Francesco de' Pazzi") (seasons 1–2)
* [Paul Rhys](/wiki/Paul_Rhys "Paul Rhys") as [Vlad Tepes](/wiki/Vlad_the_Impaler "Vlad the Impaler") (seasons 1, 3)
* [Shaun Parkes](/wiki/Shaun_Parkes "Shaun Parkes") as Solomon Ogbai, "The Abyssinian" (seasons 1–2)
* [Vincent Riotta](/wiki/Vincent_Riotta "Vincent Riotta") as Duke [Federico da Montefeltro](/wiki/Federico_da_Montefeltro "Federico da Montefeltro") (seasons 1–2)

## Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Da_Vinci%27s_Demons&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Casting")]

Tom Riley was the first actor to be cast in the series, as *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* announced he had landed the role of Leonardo da Vinci. Goyer and managing director Carmi Zlotnik revealed he was cast as he could portray the character with many dimensions that would appeal to a worldwide audience.[[11]](#cite_note-11) They later reported that Laura Haddock had been cast in the female lead as [Lucrezia Donati](/wiki/Lucrezia_Donati "Lucrezia Donati").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Da_Vinci%27s_Demons&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Production")]

The series marks the first collaboration between Starz and [BBC Worldwide](/wiki/BBC_Worldwide "BBC Worldwide"), following a new production agreement. Fox International Channels picked up the series for global distribution.[[2]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Reporter-2)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The show was created by Goyer, who directed the first two episodes and wrote several others along writers such as [Scott Gimple](/wiki/Scott_Gimple "Scott Gimple"), [Brian Nelson](/wiki/Brian_Nelson_(screenwriter) "Brian Nelson (screenwriter)") and [Joe Ahearne](/wiki/Joe_Ahearne "Joe Ahearne").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Filming for the series took place in the United Kingdom, at [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea"), [Neath](/wiki/Neath "Neath"), [Port Talbot](/wiki/Port_Talbot "Port Talbot") and [Margam Castle](/wiki/Margam_Castle "Margam Castle") in Wales. A 265,000-square-foot (24,600 m2) studio in [Swansea Gate Business Park](/wiki/Swansea_Gate_Business_Park "Swansea Gate Business Park") was also used and several [sets](/wiki/Set_construction "Set construction") were built to resemble 15th-century [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Annie Symons was brought on board as the lead [costume designer](/wiki/Costume_designer "Costume designer").[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Bear McCreary](/wiki/Bear_McCreary "Bear McCreary") composed the [score](/wiki/Film_score "Film score") for the series, and orchestrated the [main theme](/wiki/Theme_music "Theme music") to reflect Leonardo's use of [mirror writing](/wiki/Mirror_writing "Mirror writing").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Goyer had a scene in episode five depicting a kiss between Leonardo and [Jacopo Saltarelli](/wiki/Jacopo_Saltarelli "Jacopo Saltarelli") filmed in secret as he feared network interference.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Goyer revealed to *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* at the [New York Comic Con](/wiki/New_York_Comic_Con "New York Comic Con") that season two would also be set in South America and would feature [Machu Picchu](/wiki/Machu_Picchu "Machu Picchu") and the [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire"). Goyer claimed that this was justified by "new research" that has revealed Chinese and European explorers may have arrived in the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") earlier than was originally believed.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Sexuality of Leonardo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Da_Vinci%27s_Demons&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sexuality of Leonardo")]

There is widespread belief that Leonardo was primarily if not exclusively sexually attracted to and involved with men. Goyer acknowledged this and said that the show would not shy away from the subject.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Riley cited that because the speculation exists it is something that should be honoured.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In a later interview with the gay-interest website [The Backlot](/wiki/TheBacklot.com "TheBacklot.com"), Riley expressed his hope that "The Tower" episode would address concerns about the show's depiction of Leonardo's sexuality in a way that was satisfactory and respectful to any historical beliefs.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Da_Vinci%27s_Demons&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Reception")]

Season 1 has received favourable reviews from critics. It holds a 75% approval rating on aggregate review site [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), based on 32 collected critic reviews, with an average score of 6.1/10. The sites consensus reads: "Despite its preposterous plotting and lack of historical accuracy, *Da Vinci's Demons* is energetic, enjoyable escapist television."[[50]](#cite_note-50) It also holds a [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") score of 62 out of 100, based on 27 critics reviews, indicating "generally favorable reviews".[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Da_Vinci%27s_Demons&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Accolades")]

*Da Vinci's Demons* received three nominations for [Outstanding Main Title Design](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Main_Title_Design "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Main Title Design"), [Outstanding Main Title Theme Music](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Main_Title_Theme_Music "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Main Title Theme Music") and [Outstanding Special Visual Effects](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Special_Visual_Effects "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Special Visual Effects") at the [65th Primetime Creative Arts Emmy Awards](/wiki/65th_Primetime_Creative_Arts_Emmy_Awards "65th Primetime Creative Arts Emmy Awards"). The series won Main Title Design and Main Title Theme Music, but lost Visual Effects to the [Cinemax](/wiki/Cinemax "Cinemax") series *[Banshee](/wiki/Banshee_(TV_series) "Banshee (TV series)")*.[[52]](#cite_note-52)
